headline: March inflation: Egg prices may be down, but are other costs moving?
summary: Morning Brief Host Julie Hyman and Yahoo Finance Head of News Myles Udland go through various consumer categories and examine how March inflation pressures have pushed their prices higher. Last month's Consumer Price Index survey (CPI) reported to have risen 0.9% month-over-month and 3.3% year-over-year (vs. estimates of 3.4%). Core CPI — which excludes food and energy costs — saw inflation rise 0.2% monthly (vs. estimates of 0.3%) and 2.6% annually (vs. estimates of 2.7%) in March.

raw.headline: March inflation: Egg prices may be down, but are other costs moving?

headline: Anthropic’s New AI Model Triggered an Emergency Banking Meeting. Why It’s a Catalyst for Cyber Stocks.
summary: Cybersecurity stocks could be set for a massive boost from risks tied to Anthropic’s latest advances in artificial intelligence, which have stoked concerns in Washington and on Wall Street that a new generation of hackers could test the defenses of the country’s biggest banks.  Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, as well as Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, arranged an emergency meeting of top bank bosses earlier this week, Bloomberg reported on Friday, to address the risks raised by the limited release of Anthropic’s newest AI model, known as Claude Mythos.  Each of the lenders invited, according to the report, are part of the so-called Big 8 group of Global Systemically Important Banks, which the Fed deems crucial to broader financial system risks and imposes higher capital buffer rules as backstop.

raw.headline: Anthropic’s New AI Model Triggered an Emergency Banking Meeting. Why It’s a Catalyst for Cyber Stocks.
raw.summary: Cybersecurity stocks could be set for a massive boost from risks tied to Anthropic’s latest advances in artificial intelligence, which have stoked concerns in Washington and on Wall Street that a new generation of hackers could test the defenses of the country’s biggest banks.  Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, as well as Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, arranged an emergency meeting of top bank bosses earlier this week, Bloomberg reported on Friday, to address the risks raised by the limited release of Anthropic’s newest AI model, known as Claude Mythos.  Each of the lenders invited, according to the report, are part of the so-called Big 8 group of Global Systemically Important Banks, which the Fed deems crucial to broader financial system risks and imposes higher capital buffer rules as backstop.

headline: TSMC revenue surges 35% on AI chip demand
summary: STORY: :: TSMCThe world's largest contract chipmaker, TSMC, saw first-quarter revenue surge 35% thanks to the AI boom. Revenue from January-March hit $35.7 billion, beating market forecasts on surging interest in artificial intelligence applications.Friday's results are in line with TSMC's January guidance of up to to $35.8 billion on its last earnings call.The tech titan will report full first-quarter earnings on April 16, including an updated current quarter and full year outlook.The company has been a major beneficiary of advances in AI.That boom has more than offset a tapering-off in pandemic-led demand for chips used in consumer electronics like tablets.TSMC's customers include Nvidia and Apple.:: TSMCIts shares closed up more than 2% on Friday.

raw.headline: TSMC revenue surges 35% on AI chip demand

headline: TSMC's Q1 revenue jumps 35% y/y, beats market forecast
summary: TSMC, the world's ‌largest contract ‌chipmaker, reported on Friday ​first-quarter revenue of T$1.134 trillion ($35.71 billion), beating the market ‌forecasts and ⁠up 35% on the year ⁠ago period on surging interest ​in artificial ​intelligence (AI) ​applications. An LSEG ‌SmartEstimate, drawn from 20 analysts, had predicted first-quarter revenue of T$1.125 trillion. Taiwan ‌Semiconductor Manufacturing Co (TSMC) ​is ​a ​major supplier ‌to companies including Nvidia ​and ​Apple.

headline: Apple Towson Closure Puts Retail Labor Relations In Investor Focus
summary: Apple is closing its first unionized U.S. retail store in Towson, Maryland, citing deteriorating mall conditions and the exit of other retailers. The Towson store had been a milestone location for organized labor within Apple’s U.S. retail network. Employees are not being offered direct transfers as part of the closure plan, which affects how the move is being viewed by staff and labor organizers. For investors following NasdaqGS:AAPL, this development sits alongside Apple’s core business...
